question: Tell me about a time you went above and beyond what was asked.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, after finishing my assigned backend work, I saw that onboarding the rest of the team was still taking too long. My responsibility was to do extra work that materially helps, not just work that looks impressive. I built a small internal tool or guide that removed the repeated friction and documented how to use it. The result was that the team moved faster afterward because the extra work solved a real bottleneck.
follow_up_question: What happened because of your actions, and can you quantify it if possible?

feedback.interviewer_reply: Good answer. I can follow the story, and one more concrete outcome would make it stronger.
feedback.overall: This is a solid foundation, but it would be stronger with a clearer result and a bit more precision.

question: Give an example of a time your work had a measurable impact on the business.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, a recurring issue in our student project workflow kept wasting time and causing avoidable rework. My responsibility was to connect technical work to a metric that matters. I identified the root cause, changed the workflow or system behavior, and tracked the before-and-after effect. The result was that the result was measurable in time saved, reliability improved, or incidents reduced.

question: Describe a time you delivered results under pressure or tight constraints.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, our team had one night left to stabilize the demo after a late integration failure. My responsibility was to deliver the core outcome without getting distracted by lower-value work. I cut scope, protected the essential flow, and coordinated the team around the smallest set of tasks that would make the result credible. The result was that we delivered something polished enough to meet the moment instead of missing it while chasing completeness.

question: Tell me about a time you had to balance multiple deadlines at once.
candidate_answer: Last semester, I was taking two project-heavy CS classes while also serving as logistics lead for a student hackathon. In the same week, I had a systems project milestone, an algorithms assignment, and vendor confirmations due for the event. I mapped every deliverable by deadline and effort, identified which tasks required coordination with other people, and blocked my week around those first. I also delegated one vendor follow-up to another organizer after sharing the context. I submitted both assignments on time, finalized the vendor list before the event, and avoided the last-minute scramble we had in the previous semester.
follow_up_question: What did you intentionally choose not to do that week so you could focus on the highest-priority work?

feedback.interviewer_reply: This is a strong prioritization answer because it shows a clear system, not just hard work.
feedback.overall: Strong answer. You explain the competing demands, your prioritization method, and the result clearly.

task.feedback: Your obligation across classes and event work is explicit.

action.feedback: You show a deliberate prioritization process and smart delegation.

result.feedback: The outcome is clear; one more measurable detail would make it even stronger.

strengths: You demonstrate an actual prioritization framework.
You show judgment about coordination risk and delegation.

feedback.improved_answer: Last semester, I was juggling two project-heavy CS classes while serving as logistics lead for a student hackathon. In one week, I had a systems project milestone, an algorithms assignment, and multiple vendor confirmations due. My job was to keep all three moving without missing anything important. I listed every deliverable by deadline and effort, prioritized the items that depended on other people first, and blocked time for the remaining coursework around them. I also delegated one vendor follow-up to another organizer after sharing the context and expected outcome. As a result, I submitted both assignments on time, finalized the vendor list before the event, and avoided the last-minute scramble we had dealt with the previous semester. The experience taught me that good prioritization is about sequencing and coordination, not just working longer hours.

question: Tell me about a time you had to learn something quickly in order to deliver.
candidate_answer: In my database class, my team decided to deploy our project with Docker even though I had never used it before. I volunteered to own the deployment because our previous local setup was inconsistent across machines. Over one weekend, I worked through the official docs, built a simple container for our app, and documented the commands the rest of the team needed. I also added a short troubleshooting section after hitting a dependency issue on Windows. By the next class, everyone could run the same environment, which saved setup time and let us spend more of our meetings on features instead of fixing local issues.
follow_up_question: How did you make sure you were learning the right amount instead of getting lost in the tool itself?

feedback.interviewer_reply: This is a strong example because you connect rapid learning directly to delivery impact.
feedback.overall: Strong answer. You show initiative, a practical learning approach, and a clear team benefit.

task.feedback: You clearly owned deployment and environment consistency.

action.feedback: You explain how you learned and immediately applied the new tool.

result.feedback: The result is concrete and tied to team productivity.

feedback.improved_answer: In my database class, my team decided to deploy our project with Docker even though I had never used it before. After hitting a dependency issue on Windows, I added a short troubleshooting guide as well. As a result, everyone could run the same environment by the next class, which cut setup issues during team meetings and let us focus on building features. The experience taught me to learn new tools just deeply enough to unblock the real deliverable, then capture that knowledge so the whole team benefits.

feedback.improved_answer: During my internship, our sprint priorities changed quickly because new information made the original plan less valuable. The experience taught me that priority shifts feel manageable once the reasoning and next steps are both visible.

question: Tell me about a time you faced a setback while working toward a deadline.
candidate_answer: Last semester I was leading the front-end part of a hackathon project, and one of our core integrations failed the night before judging. I was responsible for getting the demo working. I quickly cut scope, replaced the broken live integration with a mocked response, and coordinated with my teammate so we could still show the user flow end to end. We finished the demo and were able to present a polished prototype the next day.
follow_up_question: How did you decide what to cut and what to keep when time was limited?

feedback.interviewer_reply: Good structure and strong prioritization under pressure. The answer would be stronger with more reflection and a clearer final impact.
feedback.overall: Good structure and strong prioritization under pressure. The answer would be stronger with more reflection and a clearer final impact.

feedback.improved_answer: At a hackathon last semester, I led the front-end work for our prototype. The night before judging, one of our live integrations stopped working, which put the demo at risk. My responsibility was to make sure we could still present a complete user experience the next morning. I quickly reduced scope, replaced the failing integration with a mocked response, and coordinated with my teammate so the demo flow remained consistent from start to finish. As a result, we delivered a polished prototype on time, received positive feedback from judges on usability, and still placed in the top group of teams. The experience taught me to separate must-have functionality from nice-to-have features when a deadline is fixed.

question: Describe a conflict with a coworker and how you resolved it.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, a teammate and I kept stepping on each other's changes because our task boundaries were unclear. My responsibility was to resolve the conflict while protecting delivery. I asked for a direct conversation, clarified responsibilities, and agreed on a handoff and review process for overlapping work. The result was that the tension dropped, the workstream became smoother, and we stopped reworking each other's changes.
follow_up_question: What happened because of your actions, and can you quantify it if possible?

question: Tell me about a time you had to work with a difficult stakeholder.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, a student organization sponsor kept changing expectations for a project portal without explaining the priority behind each request. My responsibility was to understand the real concern and reset expectations. I translated the requests into priorities, walked through the impact of each change, and proposed a ranked roadmap instead of reacting request by request. The result was that the stakeholder stopped changing direction every few days and we delivered the highest-value improvements first.

I translated the requests into priorities, walked through the impact of each change, and proposed a ranked roadmap instead of reacting request by request. As a result, the stakeholder stopped changing direction every few days and we delivered the highest-value improvements first. The experience taught me that difficult stakeholders become easier when I surface the real priorities underneath the requests. Looking back, I would probably tell the story more directly.

question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision. How did you handle it?
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, a teammate wanted to remove validation checks to speed up delivery right before our demo. question: Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a teammate.
candidate_answer: During my software engineering class, my teammate and I disagreed on how to split up the backend work for our final project. I was responsible for the API layer, and he wanted to change the database schema late in the sprint. I scheduled a short meeting, walked through the risks, proposed freezing the schema for the current sprint, and documented a plan for the next sprint so we could still evaluate his idea later. We shipped on time, and our demo worked without breaking the existing endpoints.
follow_up_question: If the teammate had pushed back harder, what would you have done next?

feedback.interviewer_reply: This is a solid STAR answer with clear ownership and action. The main thing missing is a more explicit measurable result.
feedback.overall: This is a solid STAR answer with clear ownership and action. The main thing missing is a more explicit measurable result.

situation.feedback: Clear context. You explained the project, the disagreement, and why it mattered.

task.feedback: Good. Your responsibility for the API layer is stated clearly.

action.feedback: Strong. You explained the meeting, your reasoning, and the compromise you proposed.

result.feedback: Decent but still weakly quantified. 'Shipped on time' is helpful, but add a stronger metric if possible.

strengths: You focused on your own contribution.
You framed the conflict as a problem-solving moment instead of blaming the other person.
improvements: Add a measurable outcome such as team grade, bug count, or time saved.
End with a short reflection about what you learned.
feedback.improved_answer: In my software engineering class, I owned the API layer for a four-person final project. A teammate wanted to change the database schema late in the sprint, which would have broken several endpoints I had already integrated. My job was to keep the backend stable while still addressing his concern. I scheduled a quick meeting, explained the risks of changing the schema that late, and proposed freezing the current design until after the demo. I also documented his proposal for the next sprint so it would still be evaluated. As a result, we delivered the demo on time, avoided last-minute regressions, and earned one of the top project scores in the class. The experience taught me to handle conflict by making tradeoffs explicit and keeping the team aligned on delivery goals.

question: Tell me about a time a teammate wasn't pulling their weight.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, one teammate stopped finishing assigned tasks during our group project and the rest of us were quietly covering for it. My responsibility was to address the issue fairly before it damaged the whole team. I checked in first to understand the blocker, then reset expectations and narrowed the next commitment to something concrete and observable. The result was that either the teammate re-engaged on a realistic scope or we escalated earlier instead of absorbing the problem silently.

question: Give an example of when you had to give critical feedback to a peer.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a peer on my team was shipping code quickly but skipping an agreed quality step that created cleanup work for others. My responsibility was to raise the issue respectfully and make it actionable. I gave the feedback privately, used recent examples, and suggested a lightweight checklist so the ask felt concrete rather than personal. question: Tell me about a time you had to work with a difficult stakeholder.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a nontechnical stakeholder wanted frequent changes but was not seeing the engineering tradeoffs or sequencing constraints. My responsibility was to understand the real concern and reset expectations. I translated the requests into priorities, walked through the impact of each change, and proposed a ranked roadmap instead of reacting request by request. The result was that the stakeholder stopped changing direction every few days and we delivered the highest-value improvements first.

question: Tell me about a time a teammate wasn't pulling their weight.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a team member repeatedly missed commitments on a shared deliverable and the impact started spreading to everyone else. My responsibility was to address the issue fairly before it damaged the whole team. I checked in first to understand the blocker, then reset expectations and narrowed the next commitment to something concrete and observable. The result was that either the teammate re-engaged on a realistic scope or we escalated earlier instead of absorbing the problem silently.

feedback.improved_answer: In a recent software engineering project, my manager told me my updates were too detailed and slowed decision-making because the blockers were buried. My responsibility was to process the feedback without defensiveness and change my behavior. The experience taught me that difficult feedback is most useful when I turn it into a repeatable habit change.

question: Tell me about a time things didn't go as planned mid-project.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, midway through the project, a core dependency failed and our original plan stopped making sense. My responsibility was to reassess the plan and protect the most valuable outcome. The result was that we delivered a narrower but reliable result instead of missing the milestone entirely.

question: Tell me about a project that failed. What did you learn?
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, our team built too much before validating the core user need, and the project did not land the way we expected. My responsibility was to own what I could have done differently and learn from it. I reviewed where our assumptions went unchecked, identified the decision points we rushed past, and changed how I approached the next project. The result was that the failure itself stood, but the lesson improved how I scoped and validated future work.

Our responsibility was to own what we could have done differently and learn from it. we reviewed where our assumptions went unchecked, identified the decision points we rushed past, and changed how we approached the next project. The experience taught us that good failure stories show ownership and changed behavior, not blame. We delivered a good outcome for the team.
follow_up_question: I want to hear your individual contribution. Which part did you personally own?

feedback.interviewer_reply: I can hear the team effort, but I want to understand what you personally drove.
feedback.overall: The answer sounds collaborative, but your individual ownership is still blurry.

question: Give an example of a technical decision you regret. What would you do differently?
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, I chose a shortcut implementation for a project because it looked faster, but it created cleanup work later. My responsibility was to explain the original logic honestly and show what I learned. I revisited the assumptions, identified why the shortcut was attractive in the moment, and changed how I now evaluate reversibility and maintenance cost. The result was that I would still explain the context, but I would choose the more maintainable option next time.
follow_up_question: What did you personally do that changed the direction of the situation?

feedback.interviewer_reply: Good answer. I can follow the story, and one more concrete outcome would make it stronger.
feedback.overall: This is a solid foundation, but it would be stronger with a clearer result and a bit more precision.

question: Tell me about a time you made a mistake. How did you handle it?
candidate_answer: In a data structures assignment, I submitted a version of my code that passed my local tests but failed the grader because I had overlooked an edge case with empty input. As soon as I saw the failure, I reviewed the grader output, reproduced the issue locally, and identified the branch that assumed at least one element. I fixed the logic, added a new test for the empty case, and wrote a note to my professor explaining what I had learned before the regrade window closed. The corrected version passed, and after that I started checking my code against edge cases explicitly before every submission.
follow_up_question: What process change from that mistake still affects how you work today?

feedback.interviewer_reply: This is a strong accountability answer because you own the mistake and show what changed afterward.
feedback.overall: Strong answer. You explain the mistake clearly, take responsibility, and describe both the immediate fix and the longer-term learning.

situation.feedback: The academic setting and failure point are easy to understand.

task.feedback: Your responsibility for submission quality is clear.

action.feedback: You show both debugging steps and reflection.

result.feedback: The answer includes the corrected result and a lasting process improvement.

strengths: You take ownership without making excuses.
You connect the mistake to a practical habit change.
improvements: If useful, mention the time pressure or why you missed the edge case.
Keep the professor note brief so the result remains the focus.
feedback.improved_answer: In a data structures assignment, I submitted code that passed my local tests but failed the grader because I had missed an empty-input edge case. I was responsible for the submission quality, so once I saw the failure I reproduced the issue locally, found the branch that assumed at least one element, and fixed the logic. I also added a specific test for the empty-input case and sent my professor a short note explaining what I had learned before the regrade window closed. The corrected version passed, and since then I explicitly test edge cases like empty input and boundary values before every submission. That experience taught me that owning a mistake matters less than building a better process afterward.

question: Tell me about a time you received harsh feedback. How did you respond?

question: Tell me about a time things didn't go as planned mid-project.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a project changed shape midstream because an assumption or dependency broke, and we had to reorient quickly. I re-scoped the work, reset priorities with the team, and communicated the revised plan clearly instead of pretending the original one still worked. The result was that we delivered a narrower but reliable result instead of missing the milestone entirely.

question: Tell me about a project that failed. What did you learn?
candidate_answer: During my internship, a project I worked on missed its core goal because we optimized for implementation speed before validating assumptions. My responsibility was to own what I could have done differently and learn from it. I reviewed where our assumptions went unchecked, identified the decision points we rushed past, and changed how I approached the next project. The result was that the failure itself stood, but the lesson improved how I scoped and validated future work.

question: Tell me about a time you received difficult feedback and how you responded.
candidate_answer: During my internship, my manager told me that my status updates were too detailed and made it hard for the team to spot blockers quickly. I had been trying to be thorough, but I realized the updates were not serving the audience well. I asked for two examples of stronger updates, then started using a simple structure: what changed, current blocker, and next step. I also sent my next few updates to my manager first to make sure I was improving. Within two weeks, he told me the updates were easier to scan, and the team started resolving blockers faster during standup because the key issue was obvious.
follow_up_question: What did that feedback change about how you communicate now?

feedback.interviewer_reply: That is a strong feedback example because you show receptiveness, adjustment, and measurable improvement.
feedback.overall: Strong answer. You explain the difficult feedback clearly, show no defensiveness, and demonstrate a specific behavior change.

situation.feedback: The context and feedback are easy to understand.

task.feedback: Your responsibility to communicate effectively is clear.

action.feedback: You asked for examples, changed your process, and validated the improvement.

result.feedback: You show a clear positive change in team communication.

strengths: You show maturity by focusing on improvement instead of defensiveness.
You turned feedback into a repeatable communication pattern.
improvements: If possible, add one more concrete sign that the team benefited.
Mention what mindset helped you receive the feedback well.
feedback.improved_answer: During my internship, my manager told me that my status updates were too detailed and made it harder for the team to spot blockers quickly. I had been trying to be thorough, but I realized I was optimizing for completeness instead of usefulness. My job was to improve how I communicated so the team could make decisions faster. I asked my manager for examples of better updates, then switched to a simple format: what changed, current blocker, and next step. I also shared my next few updates with him first so I could adjust quickly. Within two weeks, he told me the updates were much easier to scan, and blockers were resolved faster in standup because the key issue was clear right away. The experience taught me to tailor communication to the audience instead of assuming more detail is always better.

I needed to improve my communication before the next class presentation. I reviewed the presentation with that feedback in mind, reduced each slide to one core takeaway, and practiced explaining the material out loud so I could focus on the narrative instead of reading text. As a result, the next presentation was easier for classmates to follow, and the professor specifically noted that the structure was much clearer. That experience taught me to optimize presentations for audience understanding rather than information density.

question: Describe a situation where you had to influence a decision you disagreed with.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, my team wanted to change the database schema right before a demo even though the API integration was already stable. My responsibility was to present a different direction without creating unnecessary conflict. I gathered examples of the regression risk, framed the tradeoffs in terms of delivery impact, and proposed a safer phased alternative. The result was that the group adopted the phased approach, met the deadline, and avoided the failure mode I had raised.

question: Tell me about a time you led a project without formal authority.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, our software engineering capstone team drifted after the first sprint because nobody owned cross-team coordination. My responsibility was to create clarity and momentum without an official lead title. I created a lightweight task board, proposed short unblocker check-ins, and used those meetings to surface ownership gaps early. The result was that the team recovered the schedule, reduced duplicate work, and delivered the agreed scope on time.

question: Give an example of when you had to rally a team around a difficult goal.

question: Tell me about a time you led a team or drove a project forward.
candidate_answer: In my software engineering course, our team fell behind after the first sprint because tasks were assigned loosely and nobody had a clear owner. I was not the official team lead, but I suggested we spend thirty minutes creating a lightweight board with owners, deadlines, and blockers for each task. I also volunteered to run a short check-in twice a week so issues would surface earlier. After we adopted that structure, we finished the next sprint on schedule and reduced duplicate work because everyone knew who owned what. By the final demo, our team had caught up and delivered all core features.
follow_up_question: How did you get buy-in from teammates if you were not the formal leader?

feedback.interviewer_reply: This is a strong leadership example because it shows initiative and influence without relying on a title.

question: Describe a situation where you had to influence a decision you disagreed with.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a cross-functional team wanted to launch a brittle implementation quickly, but I believed it would create avoidable operational risk. question: Tell me about a time you took ownership of something outside your role.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a recurring support issue was frustrating internal users, but it sat between engineering and operations and nobody owned it directly. My responsibility was to step in without creating territorial friction. I proposed a narrow fix, built the first version myself, and documented the handoff so the team could keep using it. The result was that the repeated issue stopped consuming the same time every week and the team adopted the new process.

question: Give an example of when you had to rally a team around a difficult goal.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a product team had to hit an aggressive launch window even after an important dependency slipped. My responsibility was to keep the team focused on a realistic version of success. I reframed the goal around the must-have experience, cut nonessential work, and kept everyone aligned on the next few hours of execution. The result was that we delivered a polished core experience instead of a broken ambitious one, and the team stayed engaged through the finish.

question: Tell me about a time you took ownership of a problem that was affecting other people.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a reporting bug started returning stale data to the operations team a day before their weekly review. Even though I did not write the original query, I was the engineer on call for the dashboard service. I reproduced the issue, traced it to a cache invalidation problem, and coordinated with another intern to patch the query layer and add a temporary manual refresh button. I also wrote a short incident summary so the team would understand the root cause. We restored correct data before the review, and the ops manager later said the report was accurate enough to avoid delaying the meeting.
follow_up_question: What permanent change did you make after the immediate fix?

feedback.interviewer_reply: Thanks. That answer shows clear ownership and calm execution under pressure.

question: Describe a time you had to make a technical decision with incomplete information.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, we had to choose an implementation approach before we had complete user feedback or enough time for a full spike. question: Tell me about the hardest technical problem you've solved.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, our project had a race condition that only appeared under parallel requests and none of our basic tests reproduced it. My responsibility was to find the real root cause instead of treating the symptom. I reduced the problem to a smaller repro, added targeted logging, and isolated the timing assumption that was breaking under load. The result was that the fix removed the intermittent failure and gave the team a clearer mental model of the system.

question: Tell me about a time you had to balance technical debt vs. shipping speed.
candidate_answer: In a recent software engineering project, our project needed one more feature before demo day, but the code path we wanted to extend was already messy and fragile. My responsibility was to balance delivery pressure against long-term maintainability. I scoped the minimum clean-up needed to ship safely, deferred the noncritical refactor, and documented the debt that still remained. The result was that we shipped on time without creating the worst version of the long-term maintenance problem.

question: Tell me about a time you had to move forward with unclear requirements.
candidate_answer: In a product design course, my team was asked to build a campus event app, but the requirements from our teaching assistant were intentionally broad. I was responsible for the first clickable prototype. Instead of guessing, I interviewed five students about how they currently found events, grouped their pain points, and turned those into a short list of must-have features. I reviewed that list with my team before building the prototype. Because we aligned early, we finished the prototype a week ahead of the checkpoint and received feedback that the flow felt focused instead of overloaded.
follow_up_question: What tradeoff did your team make because of what you learned in those interviews?

feedback.interviewer_reply: That is a strong ambiguity example because you created structure instead of waiting for clarity to appear.
feedback.overall: Strong answer. You show initiative, a clear decision-making process, and a concrete outcome.

situation.feedback: The ambiguous project context is easy to understand.

task.feedback: Your responsibility for the prototype is clear.

action.feedback: You explained how you gathered information and turned it into decisions.

result.feedback: You included both delivery timing and user feedback.

strengths: You showed initiative instead of waiting for perfect direction.
You grounded your decision-making in user input.
improvements: Add one sentence about a feature you deliberately excluded.
Mention how your team reacted to your recommendation.
feedback.improved_answer: In a product design course, my team was asked to build a campus event app, but the assignment intentionally left the requirements broad. I owned the first clickable prototype, so I needed a way to turn vague direction into something concrete. Instead of guessing, I interviewed five students about how they found events, grouped their pain points, and used that input to define a short list of must-have features. I then reviewed those priorities with my team before building the prototype. As a result, we aligned quickly, finished the prototype a week before the checkpoint, and got feedback that the flow felt focused rather than cluttered. The experience taught me to reduce ambiguity by creating lightweight evidence and then turning it into clear decisions.

question: Tell me about a time you had to move forward with unclear requirements.

feedback.improved_answer: In a product design course, my team was asked to build an app with intentionally broad requirements. I was responsible for the first prototype, so I needed to define a practical starting point. I interviewed several students about how they currently found events, identified the top pain points, and used those patterns to recommend three must-have features for the prototype. After aligning with my team, I built the flow around those priorities. As a result, we finished the prototype early and received feedback that it was focused and easy to use. That experience taught me to handle ambiguity by creating structure from user input instead of waiting for perfect direction.

question: Describe a time you had to make a technical decision with incomplete information.
candidate_answer: During my internship, I needed to make a technical decision even though usage patterns and stakeholder expectations were still evolving. My responsibility was to move forward with enough confidence while limiting downside. I identified the assumptions that mattered most, chose the option with the safest rollback path, and documented what signal would trigger a change. The result was that we moved forward without blocking delivery and still had a clear path to adjust when new information arrived.

question: Tell me about the hardest technical problem you've solved.
candidate_answer: During my internship, a production-adjacent service had an intermittent data inconsistency issue that was hard to reproduce and easy to dismiss. My responsibility was to find the real root cause instead of treating the symptom. I reduced the problem to a smaller repro, added targeted logging, and isolated the timing assumption that was breaking under load. The result was that the fix removed the intermittent failure and gave the team a clearer mental model of the system.

candidate_answer: During my internship, a recurring engineering process had become too complex, slow, and dependent on tribal knowledge. My responsibility was to remove unnecessary complexity without losing the important safeguards. I mapped the workflow end to end, removed redundant steps, and turned the essential parts into one documented path. The result was that the process became faster, easier to onboard to, and less likely to fail because of missed handoffs.

question: Tell me about a time you had to balance technical debt vs. shipping speed.
candidate_answer: During my internship, the team needed to ship an important capability, but a quick implementation would have deepened debt in a risky part of the codebase. My responsibility was to balance delivery pressure against long-term maintainability. I scoped the minimum clean-up needed to ship safely, deferred the noncritical refactor, and documented the debt that still remained. The result was that we shipped on time without creating the worst version of the long-term maintenance problem.
follow_up_question: What did you personally do that changed the direction of the situation?

feedback.interviewer_reply: Good answer. I can follow the story, and one more concrete outcome would make it stronger.
feedback.overall: This is a solid foundation, but it would be stronger with a clearer result and a bit more precision.
